senses.definition: In biology and mycology, the formation of spores by cutting off small parts from a spore-bearing structure.
examples: Abjunction can be observed in certain species of fungi under the microscope.
The researcher described the abjunction of spores from the main sporophore.

senses.definition: In logic, a relation where one statement does not follow from another.
examples: In this example, there is abjunction between the premises and the conclusion.
Material abjunction shows that the truth of one proposition does not imply the truth of another.

senses.definition: Plural of abjunction: separate instances or types of spore formation by cutting off parts, or of nonimplication in logic.
examples: Different fungi show different kinds of abjunctions.
The textbook lists several abjunctions between these sets of statements.

senses.definition: Relating to abjunction, especially to the cutting off of parts or the idea of nonconnection.
examples: The abjunctive process of spore separation is clearly visible.
They discussed the abjunctive relationship between the two propositions.

senses.definition: A formal or public act of stating that you no longer hold a belief, right, or claim.
examples: His abjuration of his former political beliefs surprised many of his supporters.
The court required an abjuration of any claim to the disputed land.

senses.definition: Plural of abjuration: several formal acts of giving up beliefs, rights, or claims.
examples: Throughout history there have been many forced abjurations of religious faith.
The document recorded the abjurations of title by all the claimants.

senses.definition: Expressing or involving a formal giving up of a belief, right, or claim.
examples: She read an abjuratory statement renouncing all previous alliances.
The abjuratory oath ended his claim to the throne.

senses.definition: To formally and publicly say that you no longer hold a belief, right, or claim.
examples: Under pressure, he agreed to abjure his former extremist views.
She chose to abjure her citizenship and move abroad.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of abjure: formally and publicly said that you no longer held a belief, right, or claim.
examples: He abjured his earlier statements during the hearing.
They had abjured all loyalty to the former regime.

senses.definition: The act of abjuring; a formal giving up of a belief, right, or claim.
examples: The abjurement of his title was signed before witnesses.
Her abjurement of previous convictions marked a new beginning.

senses.definition: A person who formally gives up a belief, right, or claim.
examples: The abjurer signed a declaration renouncing his former faith.
As an abjurer of violence, she spoke in favor of peaceful protest.

senses.definition: Plural of abjurer: people who formally give up beliefs, rights, or claims.
examples: The new law protected abjurers from further punishment.
Several abjurers signed the document together.

senses.definition: Third person singular of abjure: formally and publicly says that he or she no longer holds a belief, right, or claim.
examples: He abjures all forms of discrimination in his writings.
The witness abjures any connection to the group.

senses.definition: Present participle of abjure: formally and publicly saying that you no longer hold a belief, right, or claim.
examples: By abjuring his past views, he hoped to regain public trust.
She is abjuring her membership in the former party.

senses.definition: A historical official in South Asia responsible for collecting taxes on alcohol or managing state alcohol production.
examples: The abkar visited the taverns to ensure taxes were paid.
Records mention an abkar supervising the royal distillery.

senses.definition: Relating to government control or taxation of alcoholic drinks, especially in colonial South Asia.
examples: The abkari laws regulated the sale of liquor in the region.
They studied abkari policy under the colonial administration.

senses.definition: A system or department that controls and taxes alcohol production and sale.
examples: Most of the state's income came from abkari.
He worked in the abkari and inspected distilleries.

senses.definition: Another form of abkari, relating to the taxation or regulation of alcohol.
examples: Abkary regulations changed several times during that period.
They examined abkary records from the colonial era.

senses.definition: An older spelling of Abkhaz, referring to a person from Abkhazia or a member of the Abkhaz people.
examples: An abkhas merchant arrived from the Black Sea coast.
The village was mainly inhabited by abkhas families.

senses.definition: Relating to Abkhazia, its people, or its language.
examples: They collected Abkhasian folk tales for the study.
Abkhasian customs differ from those of neighboring regions.

senses.definition: A person from Abkhazia or of Abkhasian origin.
examples: An Abkhasian guided us through the mountain passes.
Several Abkhasians attended the conference on Caucasian languages.

senses.definition: Written short form of ablative, a grammatical case used in some languages.
examples: The noun appears in the abl. in this sentence.
In the table, the abl. form is listed in the final column.

senses.definition: In some Scottish dialects, a weak or worthless person or animal.
examples: They dismissed him as an ablach not fit for hard work.
The old horse had turned into an ablach by winter.

senses.definition: To stop feeding a baby or young animal with its mother's milk and start giving other food.
examples: Farmers usually ablactate the calves after a few months.
The doctor advised them to ablactate the child gradually.

senses.definition: Stopped being fed with the mother's milk and started on other food.
examples: The ablactated lambs were moved to a separate pen.
Once ablactated, the infants were given soft solid foods.

senses.definition: Stopping a baby or young animal from feeding on its mother's milk and introducing other food.
examples: They are ablactating the puppies over a two-week period.
Ablactating too quickly may upset the infant's digestion.

senses.definition: The process of stopping a baby or young animal from drinking its mother's milk and giving it other food instead.
examples: Proper ablactation is important for the child's health.
The article described traditional methods of ablactation.

senses.definition: Plural of ablactation: several cases or types of stopping milk feeding and introducing other food.
examples: The study compared ablactations in different communities.
Veterinarians recorded the timings of ablactations on the farm.

senses.definition: To remove soil from around the roots of a tree or plant, often for treatment or inspection.
examples: Gardeners ablaqueate the old trees to check for root damage.
They decided to ablaqueate the vine and improve drainage.

senses.definition: To sound loudly like a trumpet or alarm.
examples: Sirens ablare in the distance signaled the start of the drill.
The horns were ablare as the parade began.

senses.definition: Relating to or having little or no capacity for growth or development in tissue.
examples: The ablastemic cells failed to regenerate the damaged area.
Researchers studied ablastemic changes in the tissue samples.

senses.definition: A substance produced by the body that slows or stops the growth of certain tissues or organisms.
examples: The presence of ablastin was noted in the blood samples.
Scientists are investigating how ablastin affects tumor cells.

senses.definition: Lacking the ability to form buds or new growth.
examples: The ablastous tissue showed no signs of repair.
Some ablastous organisms reproduce only by other means.

senses.definition: To remove or destroy material from the surface of something, often by cutting, burning, or evaporation.
examples: Surgeons used a laser to ablate the damaged tissue.
Heat from the atmosphere causes the outer layer of the spacecraft to ablate.

senses.definition: Removed or destroyed from a surface, often by cutting, burning, or evaporation.
examples: The surgeon carefully ablated the abnormal tissue.
Most of the outer coating had been ablated during reentry.

senses.definition: Is removed or destroyed from a surface, or removes something from a surface, by cutting, burning, or evaporation.
examples: The laser ablates only the top layer of skin.
As the glacier melts and ablates, it loses mass each year.

senses.definition: Removing or destroying material from a surface by cutting, burning, or evaporation.
examples: The device is ablating small sections of the tumor.
Ablating the outer layer protects the spacecraft underneath.

senses.definition: The removal or destruction of material from the surface of something, often by surgery, heat, or friction.
examples: The patient underwent cardiac ablation to correct the irregular heartbeat.
Ablation of the shield occurs as the spacecraft reenters the atmosphere.

senses.definition: Plural of ablation: several cases or types of removal or destruction of material from a surface.
examples: Different types of cardiac ablations are available for patients.
Scientists compared ablations on the spacecraft's heat shield.

senses.definition: Tending to be carried away or removed.
examples: The ablatitious material peeled off under strong winds.
Such ablatitious layers protect the object beneath them.

senses.definition: Relating to the ablative case in grammar.
examples: The ablatival ending changes according to number and gender.
They discussed ablatival constructions in Latin poetry.

senses.definition: Relating to the ablative case in some languages, often used to show separation, source, or means.
examples: In Latin, this noun takes an ablative form after the preposition.
The textbook lists all ablative endings in the appendix.

senses.definition: A grammatical case that in some languages shows separation, source, or means.
examples: The ablative is often used to show means or instrument.
Students sometimes confuse the ablative with the dative.

senses.definition: Designed to protect by losing material from the surface, which carries away heat.
examples: The ablative shield burned away during reentry.
Engineers chose an ablative material for the spacecraft's nose.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to the ablative case or to removal from a surface.
examples: The phrase is used ablatively in this sentence.
The material erodes ablatively to carry heat away.

senses.definition: Plural of ablative: several forms or instances of the ablative case.
examples: The table lists nominatives and ablatives side by side.
Different ablatives show different functions in the text.

senses.definition: A material or device that protects something by gradually being removed from its surface, carrying heat away.
examples: Engineers tested a new ablator for the spacecraft's heat shield.
The ablator charred and peeled away during reentry.

senses.definition: Plural of ablator: materials or devices that protect objects by gradually losing material.
examples: Modern spacecraft use advanced ablators to survive reentry.
Different ablators were compared in the experiment.

senses.definition: A regular change of a vowel inside a word that marks a different grammatical form, as in sing, sang, sung.
examples: Ablaut explains the pattern in verbs like drive, drove, driven.
Students learned how ablaut operates in Indo-European languages.

senses.definition: Plural of ablaut: different regular vowel changes that mark different grammatical forms.
examples: The book lists several common ablauts in German verbs.
These verb sets show similar ablauts across related languages.

senses.definition: Burning with flames.
examples: The building was ablaze when the firefighters arrived.
Within minutes, the forest was ablaze.

senses.definition: Very bright or full of strong color or light.
examples: The sky was ablaze with stars.
In autumn, the hillsides are ablaze with red and gold leaves.

senses.definition: Filled with strong emotion, especially anger or excitement.
examples: The crowd was ablaze with excitement.
His eyes were ablaze with anger.

senses.definition: Having the power, skill, or qualities needed to do something.
examples: She is able to speak three languages.
If you are able, please come to the meeting early.

senses.definition: Very good at what you do.
examples: He is an able lawyer with many years of experience.
The company is led by a very able manager.

senses.definition: Having physical or mental abilities, often used in contrast with disabled.
examples: The building must be accessible to both abled and disabled people.
Some programs support abled students as well as those with special needs.

senses.definition: To kindle or set on fire; to make blaze.
examples: They ableezed the pile of dry leaves at dusk.
A single spark ableezed the wooden shed.

senses.definition: A papal representative or envoy sent on a special mission.
examples: The Pope appointed an ablegate to negotiate the treaty.
An ablegate arrived to deliver the official message.

senses.definition: To send a papal representative or envoy on a mission.
examples: The Pope decided to ablegate a trusted cardinal to the council.
They ablegated him to resolve the dispute.
